A fee presentation mistakes, which we are on page 37. You're following along in the song book. Number one is, of course, establishing the precedent of free. It's hard to take that back. Very difficult. So when you do free, you've got to think long and hard about it. J. Abraham has a wrap about, which is the way he handles it. He has a whole rift worth hearing about that he has decided for reasons X, Y, and Z to buy you a day of his time. And so making an exception and actually buying you his day. Really, he's doing a free day. Now, I don't like it under any circumstances, but it's a better way to say it. It ought to be the exception when there is free. Writing to assignment, we talked about that. Talking about money too soon. So that's writing in line with writing to assignment. That's the answering the fee question or disclosing the fee before there has been a diagnostic conversation. So you're now talking money too soon. So how much do you charge to do a full page magazine ad question? We'll never get an answer for big. Because it's the wrong question. A, I don't know if we're going to do a full page magazine ad. And B, I don't know if we're going to do one or we're going to do 20 variables. And I don't know what else we're going to need to do. I do projects, not pieces. So let's have the conversation about what the objective is and how we're going to get to the objective. Then let's talk about the money. But we don't want to be talking about money first. Selling to a selling mode. You have to sell without it being overtly seen and perceived and felt as selling. Which again is why diagnosis has to be part of the process. The selling mode is also the question that you just asked. Sales people typically are defensive against objections. The worst sales people in the world play ping pong with their prospect. So the prospect raises an objection. They vigorously answer the objection. Prospect raises another objection. They vigorously answer the objection. People who are really good at it can sell that way and make a living. But it's kind of an ugly process. And it is clearly selling. So the position you don't want to be in is vigorously defending yourself against objection after objection after objection. You're perceived now as selling not as consulting. We want to be perceived as consulting. So for the most part you solve it by getting all that stuff out ahead of time. And you being the one to raise it, not them raising it. Inviting negotiation. How do we invite negotiation? Well, attitudinally people do it. Occasionally it comes up. I have occasionally recently had somebody, you know, well do you negotiate fees? No. Is this negotiable? No. And there's a process for figuring out what the fee is going to be which they see the process implemented. So I'll show you that a little later. I wanted to share this. I did it on money too soon. I just got this. This is April 16th. Somebody had sent in, I asked my newsletter subscribers for stuff because I'm working on a book about price strategies. And they sent this in. This is a B2B marketer. I'm just going to read you the whole letter. But there it is. So it's a company called Weetmark. I'm not sure I even know what they do. Maybe he tells us. Finally by May 1st we were able to do it. I think it's the single most important price strategy that you teach. We took our prices off our website and stopped listing them all together. The move was a scary departure for us. But soon we were laughing all the way to the bank as we doubled sales month to month from April to May. Here's just a few of the advantages we found of not having prices listed anywhere. Number one, it encourages a consultative approach to selling rather than an order taking approach. Number two, it greatly reduces apples to apples price comparisons. Number three, it strengthens positioning in the prospect of smiling. Apprentices, we make them apply to talk to us too. And number four, it allows us to raise prices at will. But the single most important reason to take your prices off your website is that doing so allows you to choose your customers. And he's absolutely right. I mean, priceless are for pizza shops. Priceless are not for professionals. You don't walk into the Cleveland Clinic and find a price list. There may be a day, but here's how you know if you have Obamacare, if the directors to your doctor's office say, turn left at the trailer park. I actually have a whole roster of them, but we don't have to. But you don't go into the Cleveland Clinic and find a price list. That's not the way it works. And so it shouldn't work that way for us either. Problem clients, problems with clients very quickly. So one is a client that just doesn't get it. They don't understand what it is that we do and they're going to require way too much time in educating them, defending everything that you're doing. You have to make your decision about whether or not you want to be in that role or not. Number two, refusal or inability to work with you as agree. Key words there are as agree. This whole agreements process with the client. And again, I'm going to give you contracts. I'm going to give you correspondence. We're going to get there. This whole agreements process with the client of here's how things are going to work. It's very important to have it so you can refer to it when they start to deviate. See, trying to fix them has bad behavior surface that weren't discussed to begin with. It can't be done. It's not even fair. So you want an agreements process. If you're not familiar with it from the personal development side. It's an old book, but I suggest you find it. It's called the book of est. And it is an inside account of going through the S program. You laugh. By the way, why do you laugh? Which? Oh, well, yeah, but that's its point. Werner and I are getting our hair cut next to each other and efforts hair salon in San Francisco. First time I met and I said, I knew his predecessors. So we're going to have our men had asked if you don't. And so I said, off the record, some of some of your business asked in 20 words or less. He says, we preach independence and we breathe. They mastered sell it by Zealot, but maybe better than anybody else. But anyway, you should get the book of est. It's irrelevant what you think of est. But they probably had the best agreements process. Really the first three hours of the seminar was the agreements process. And it's in there almost word for word. So it's worth reading and seeing a good agreement process. The agreements process with the client is important and it needs to be paper trailed, not just said. So correspondence of contracts, you'll see it because then when you start to deviate, you get to play fair. You can say, wait a minute, here's what we agreed to. Right. Interference by committee, which has to be dealt with in your agreements process. Because this is one of the most common problems is you thought you were dealing with John and now all of a sudden John's got a whole closet full of networks who are weighing in on the process. It might be his board of directors. It might be his mother-in-law. It might be who knows, but all of a sudden you're not dealing with John. You're dealing with some committee and you and maybe you can't even get to the committee. Well, that's no good. So that has to be controlled from the beginning. Personality conflicts, conflicts of interest, of course, are legitimate. If you take work outside your ability to perform, if you really are in over your head, that's a problem. Dealing with clients of questionable integrity in their relationships with you and people like you. Not necessarily questionable integrity in general. That would rule out 30% of everybody I've ever worked for. So I'm not running a church. I mean, so, but there has to be honor among thieves, if you will. And so like if they arrive at my doorstep now, cold, I kind of know they've got problems somewhere else because I'm the most expensive guy on the planet. So the reason they're there, right, is because four other people have fired. So my deal is you need to have witness tests. And one is, you know, if I can't find three people who've done business with you, who say they would do business with you again, we're not going to do business. Right. Because I kind of know I'm not going to be the one to cure all the bad behavior. Clients with actual or probable legal problems. You'll see in my contracts language about all this, but you make your own decisions. Because you can wind up sitting in a room with the attorney general of Iowa, having a discussion at some length about your work with client A. And so you make your own decisions about that, but you ought to be aware of it, cognizant of it, careful of it. Clients who operate under FTC consent decrees. I have three of them right now. It's okay as long as I know. But in some respects, it's actually better because they're constrained from doing certain things they would normally like to do. But if you don't know, that's a whole other issue altogether. Emergencies. Clients with emergencies. You can get a lot of money from them, but you have very low likelihood of success. And very low likelihood of long-term relationships. So I told her a story this morning. I'm not going to take time to tell it now, but the 911 call, because they are in trouble for one reason or another. You really, you have to make conscious choices about all that. It usually requires you to drop everything else you're doing in order to perform well. And you almost know going in, the result won't be good. The result will just be not as bad as it would be. So it's the stimulus argument, right? We can't prove it did anything, but imagine how much worse everything would be if we hadn't done it. That's the position you'll be in. And so you better be well paid for it. What are you doing? Someone's retreating from a deal. We'll get to that in correspondence. I'm going to skip the entire, I'm just going to skip a bunch of stuff. Jump to page 44. This is important. So there's what I call in every business and in every task, there's a good enough spot. So first of all, not all GE spots are in the same place. This is extremely frustrating to males. One or two, huh? Okay. Well, they're not. So forget copy. Think about a simple business. So Southwest Airlines has a different GE spot than the other. You have certain expectations of Southwest about which you will control your satisfaction. And you're okay with being herded on like cattle. And the stampede at the gate and the little colored passes. And they only give you peanuts and nothing else. And you don't care about it. Because you would set up in advance. Southwest is all about getting where you're going on time. That's the deal. That's their GE spot. And almost nothing else matters. You fly first class on American. You have an entirely different set of expectations. Therefore they're stuck with an entirely different GE spot. So all businesses have it, right? So you have a set of expectations. And you have an entirely different GE spot. So all businesses have it, right? If we were in a Ritz, you would get Ritz Carlton as a different GE spot than this hotel. In terms of what's in the room, the personnel, everything, right? So it's situational. It's never absolute. The most important thing is there is little benefit to you in achieving more than good enough results. Unless your royalties are tied in to maximum results. Otherwise, and even then sometimes. So if you spend too much time achieving plus-issue something. So cost per sale is 200 bucks. And you get it down to 180. If you spend a ton of time getting it down to 165. But, and now don't move the client on to six other opportunities that were bigger and better opportunities in his business. And therefore better opportunities for you to work on. And he was happy with 180, right? You've accomplished nothing with all that effort to get it down to 165. They're not even. He's making plenty of money at 165. He's happy at 165. He thinks you're a genius at 165. Move on to the next thing. Especially if the next thing is an even bigger, better opportunity. So there may be a share further away from the furnace in the afterlife for maximum results in every situation. I don't know, but there's no real reward here. Unless it is directly affecting your money. And even then, you have to be careful not to be getting too much over here at the expense of not also going over here. And going over here. And going over here. And going over here. So right now I have a client. They came to me for front end. They want to improve their front end results first transaction, conversion of custom. The big opportunity is in. They have a 35% refund. Now they're making a ton of money. So they like are ignoring it. The big opportunity is there. Huge. Because they're not doing anything to control it. So with a series of stick letters, sales letters that remind you why you bought it in the first place and make you happy about buying it. Right, I'm probably cut that to 20%. There's 15 points there. And it's a ton of money. So all I'm going to do with the front end is enough to improve where they're at. I want to be done with that in a week. Because I want to spend my time over here. Now, if I spent four months and I did more research and I did more work and I did more variables and we tested more variables. Could I improve the front end even more than I'm going to? Yes. And maybe if we have a long enough relationship I'll get all the way back around. But right now I want to do just enough to satisfy them on that count. So we can focus on this where there's an enormous amount of money to be had. And guess what? On the front end, because the cost of acquisition is very high anyway, the kind of business they're in, the size of the volume they do, they can only give me a 1% royalty. On this, that they don't think is any big opportunity anyway, we're splitting the reduction in the refund. Well, if I'm right about 15 points, 7 points of that's mine. Now, eventually they're going to want to buy it out, which I'll show you in the contract, which is fine. But for them and for me it's better to go spend the time over here than it is to spend the time over here. So good enough, good enough, right? Okay, I want to start showing you some things. So on page 46, you have a page. It looks like this. For the sake of speed, because you haven't been in your book, I'm not going to put them up on this thing. Okay? You can't see them up there anyway. You're going to write along with being in the book and I'm going to look at my notes. Is that okay with everybody? Okay, so page 46. This is the basic language if we're going to have a consulting relationship. Now you can read all this at home. I'm just going to call attention to a couple of things if you want to mark them, you can. So number one essentially says no warranty of non-disclosure. So item A is no warranty of non-disclosure or confidentiality. That's important because signing non-disclosure agreements and signing confidentiality agreements is an invitation to buy. It's something to be litigated against. That's what it is. And I don't know about you, but I haven't seen anybody yet with an original idea. They all think they got, but they don't. I got 50 duplicates in my file drawer. And too much stuff crosses my desk. This comes up a lot, by the way, for me. And the answer always is, I don't sign them. I don't agree to them. Here's why I don't. Here's the paper trail that not only didn't I sign yours, you signed mine that says no agreement on disclosure or confidentiality. Number two, indemnity. The indemnity clause essentially says whatever the hell they do with what I give them, it's on them. It's not on me. And if anybody asks me, so if I do have to have that conversation with the Attorney General in Iowa, or the head of consumer protection in Tupelo, or whatever, they're paying all the costs for it. It's on them. Three is maybe the most important thing here. This is, it's headlined in the word access. The real word here is limitation. Right? So this spells out the rules of the road for how we're going to work together and how we're going to communicate. The most important thing is, rarely available on short notice. So Charlie's now frustrated that he can't pick up the call, or he can't want me at 10 o'clock, he can't get me at 10.05. Wait a minute, Charlie. You signed this thing for Christ's sake. Not only did I tell you, I gave it to you in writing, and you signed it. No right to complain, right? I don't know, I guess so. So the rules of the road are spelled out. Conflicts of interest. This is a disclosure paragraph. This says, if I bring him Dean, and I turn him over to Dean, I'm getting a check from Dean, and I'm not keeping it a secret. I may tell him, I may not. But I've told him here, and I've told him then that I may tell him, or I may not. So there's no secrets. The next page is a version of the same thing for the day. And so this is my diagnostic day, or any consultative day. And it includes that they can't screw with the schedule. So the first thing says, once you bought it, Bubba, you own it. I don't want to hear that you suddenly had to go to a wedding, and we need to reschedule. No ducks. So everybody knows that ahead of time. You bought a day, and you own it. It also has my right, however, to screw with the schedule. Two, it tells them they should record the day. So I have clients all the time. Now they don't have to show up with recording equipment, if you don't tell them to. They show up without a pencil. I mean, it's like, I'm here, well, wonderful. Seven hours from now, what are we going to have here? Right? And from my standpoint, I want to record it because if I'm going to do work for them, I want the recording of the whole day we just spent together. Because I said a lot of smart stuff that I don't remember that I need to use to do the work for them. So they're told to bring recording equipment. Same absence of warranties. The day is spelled out. They also see that the details of the day tells them exactly where they're supposed to stay, where they're supposed to be, when they're going to get picked up, when they're going to get plunked back out of there. So there's no questions about that. The last one tells them how to get me stuff in advance to look at. All right. Then there's the issue of questionnaires. I wasn't going to do that. So page 48. So questionnaires, a lot of people use them. I haven't used them in a long time. I mean, I have it in my head. And I'll sometimes prepare notes ahead of time with questions I want to ask, but I don't have a lot of questions. But there's a lot of good reasons to do it. Especially if you are a little light on authority, making them do the questionnaire imposes some authority. It also exposes inadequacies. So if you structure it right, it's a great way to make the client feel even more inadequate than they were when they called you in the first place, which is good. It also makes them invest in the right way. And the more time they already have invested in you, the more likely they are to hire you, the less likely they are to walk away empty. Structured right, it demonstrates how smart you are. And then there's the issue of the questionnaires. So if you're a little light on authority, you're going to have to look at the questionnaires. And then there's the issue of the questionnaires. Structured right, it demonstrates how smart you are. And there's even some reassurance to the fact that you even have a process. So especially if you're light on authority, the very fact that you have a process is kind of reassuring. We're not going to go through them. I've given you two of them. So I've given you the one I built for our independent business advisors to use, our Glaser Kennedy independent business advisors. And it goes from 49 to 68. And it really is a variation of the one I used to use many, many moons ago. And I've also, on page 69, I've given you the critique form that I use. And our independent business advisors use when we do a critique for somebody. So for example, some of my products, some of my books have a critique coupon in it. Because we're going to get to it in about two minutes. So starting on page 74, you have my little communication control forms. I mix them up because I'm dealing with same people all the time. And I think it's nice to be amusing. But so the first one is sort of my answer to email. All three of them mostly control the FedEx. I found over the years that staff sometimes surprisingly doesn't take FedEx into the person. There's a, we never got that. Almost never has FedEx actually failed to deliver it or lost it. If it doesn't get there, they could call FedEx and chase it down. They don't need to bother me. And now they're looking for it. So those are little forms. Now, the thing I've given you next, let me find those documents because I need to do them. I need one of those. I need one of those for some reason. I don't seem to have mine for some reason. I don't know why that is. So these don't have page numbers. So you're going to have to stay with me. I'll show it to you. So this one, which you'll see, urgent response for Delan. So this is a response to somebody who wanted a consulting day on very short notice. And so this addresses that. And I'm looking for something that I don't have. So this is from 2005, incidentally. So this starts out by telling them they can't have a short notice day. Truth is, I want to give this guy a short notice day. I know I'm in the past and there's a lot of work there to be. So it starts off by telling them there's no way to have a short notice day. And he's going to screw up my rotation on and on and on. Then it takes it away further by telling them I got a client that might be in conflict that I wouldn't work with him unless I cleared him with the client first. Now I happen to know the client isn't going to say no. I've raised the issue anyway. Then I lay down the fee plus I want some additional stuff because I'm screwing up my rotation and I'm meeting with him on short notice. So I want to bake myself in this promotion. Then I tell him I'm not going to have any time to take on any copywriting assignments. Whatever we get done during the day is what we get done during the day. But that's going to make him do is try and kill me to get me to take the copywriting assignment at the end of the day. And pretty much fee resistance will be non-existent because I've told him he can't have it. Then at the end I offer to refer him to somebody to solve his problem if he does want to solve his problem with me. I think the first project was $45,000 or something. He wound up spending a couple hundred. $06000, you have one that looks like this. $0700, $06000. So this is a client who has sent me stuff. The greatest invention since sliced bread in dental care. He's kind of cold. He came to be cold. So you'll see that the letter deals with, again, not being very available, essentially. And then making it very clear that this isn't going to be about the day. I'm only going to liberate a day from him if he's prepared to spend X amount of money on his project on the back end. So I've laid the money right out there to start with. And as a third alternative, I offer to refer him to somebody who could help him if he doesn't want me. The commonality here in those two and in many others is a lot of takeaway. Some of it essentially is real. I mean, the guy was screwed up my vacation. And I did have to deal with the explanation to Carla why there's a day less vacation, which is painful. So that's real. The fact that I'm not going to take this other guy unless he is prepared to spend X, that's real. But it's also manipulative. It is also about controlling things from the beginning, like fee resistance. Like, well, those three days don't work for me. Can I have, no, so that's all headed off in advance. Here is one. Your next one should be 5306. Okay. So this is somebody, they came for a day, we worked for a day. They sent me some follow up stuff. And so this is now response to the queries they raised in their follow up. Now what's important here, the reason I'm showing it to you, is the second paragraph. They asked me a question about who they should go get to do their website work that we discussed during the day they were with me. So I tell them two places to go see it done well, like we talked about during that day. I then tell them that recommending somebody is a bit of a problem. I'm going to put out a few feelers this week and try and get a few options to you late next week. This is a common ingredient you're going to see now as we go along. I don't want to give them everything all at once. A, I want to make it look like what I'm doing for them is more difficult than it is. Like I put myself out more than I am. Why? Because it obligates them. And so like, here's another example. When they come to my house for a consulting meeting, so I got all my stuff there, everything. If I know you're coming and you're in XYZ business and I've seen your stuff even before the diagnostic conversation, I kind of know what we're going to talk about. So I could neatly organize in the conference room these samples out of all my stuff that I'm going to want to show you as we go along during the course of the day. However, that's not as impressive as going back into the storeroom and rooting around and finding something for you. Essentially going out hunting, killing it, dragging it back for us to eat. That's that they like better. Because I'm going to all this trouble and I know right where it is. So I could have done it ahead of time, but they're happier because I don't do it ahead of time. Same thing here. So now a week later I get back to them with information that I have hunted and killed for him and had to go to trouble to get. And he's happier and he's more obligated and he is now more likely to be compliant and do business. I could have put it this way. I just didn't. So it's posture. I admit. This involves the same client. This guy was a referral. So also then on 530.06 goes the report to the person who referred. Which in this case is Greg Ranker at Guthrie Ranker. So I'm flipping with Greg all the time. So this is to Greg, the king of all you can see. And so the first paragraph is a report on these guys he referred to me. Which they're kind of nutballs. So I tell them they're kind of nutballs and I didn't take them as clients. But here's what I've done for them and thank you very much anyway. And then I use that as excuse to get into something else with Greg. Point being if you do get a referral you should always report. They want to know. And they deserve to know. And you want to control what they're told. You don't want the person they refer especially if it's somebody things didn't go well or you didn't accept. You want to be the one to report that. I assume was not for April Fool's Day No need to be a diplomat got it out Second paragraph says I'm going to address this stuff, but the contract isn't a proposal It's my standard agreement every client accepts So that's like the first volley across the bow So the first complaint My answer is essentially if I got something that already has worked six times and I can adapt it for you You ought to be happy about that not grumpy about why do you want me creating an experiment from scratch? When I can give you something that works So that's my answer to that The second one is the answer to the little clause that says I may use other people which is basically says I don't however Ultimately if I have a secret room full of incredibly gifted monkeys working just for bananas who managed to come up with the greatest Add ever and I merely apply a coat of polish to it and add my name. What do you care? What you want for me is best possible outcome how I get to it is irrelevant Now really it's kind of hard to argue with that isn't it? If you want an ad that works and I got a horse in the barn Who you can who will tap on a typewriter with his hoof and produce an ad that works? What do you use? Who cares right? They are coming to me because they got problems There's a little sale paragraph the second paragraph Now the next paragraph because of the way they phrase their inquiry Okay, to be clear. I do not operate as a copywriter given assignments So the inquiry that came to me was because what Halbert did form way back when is he wrote him an ad and a tear sheet and Results are finally starting to decline after all this time What they want is a new ad and a new tear sheet and how much will you charge that sort of was the query So this is the answer I Do not operate as a copywriter given assignments rather is a strategic marketing advisor who often but not always also writes copy Now there's more sales right But I've switched to selling the diagnostic day Which is what I want to sell So I switched to selling the diagnostic day.
